input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText:
Good. I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks `AverageWordLength`, `WordLengthHistogram`, and `UniqueWordCount` across both .NET 9 and .NET 10. Both runtimes are available.

I'll create a benchmark project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0, uses BenchmarkDotNet, and benchmarks the three methods.

Let me create the benchmark project.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-ff468851b9734049a1d4fc421a35770e/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-ff468851b9734049a1d4fc421a35770e/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-ff468851b9734049a1d4fc421a35770e/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 might not support .NET 10 yet. Let me use a custom runtime definition approach, or just use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute approach with custom moniker. Actually, let me try using `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion` or just define the runtime manually.

Let me check the BenchmarkDotNet API for creating custom runtimes.
